core_intuitions: A GPU has two mismatched speed limits — peak FLOP/s (pi) and peak HBM bytes/s (beta) — and their ratio is the ridge point I* = pi/beta; you classify any kernel by comparing its arithmetic intensity to that single number.

Arithmetic intensity is set by REUSE: element-wise ops and matrix-vector (GEMV) products use each byte read from HBM only ~once (low I, memory-bound), while matrix-matrix (GEMM) products reuse each loaded tile across many outputs (high I, compute-bound).

Single-stream decode is a GEMV that streams the ENTIRE weight set from HBM to produce one token (I~1, bandwidth-bound); batching many requests lets one weight read serve many tokens, raising effective intensity above the ridge — this is the whole economic case for batching.

Transformer forward FLOPs are dominated by the d^2 terms (FFN 16d^2 + QKVO 8d^2); attention scores are ~4sd (proportional to s^2 over a sequence) and only dominate the FLOP budget at very long context — yet can dominate runtime because the score matrix is memory-bound (FLOPs are not time).
Two memorized constants power all capacity math: training ~6N*D FLOPs, inference ~2N FLOPs/token; MFU = 6N*tok_s / (GPUs*pi) is the north-star efficiency metric (healthy large runs ~40-55%).

Every optimization is a move on the roofline: memory-bound kernels go faster only by raising intensity / moving fewer bytes; compute-bound kernels by faster math or fewer FLOPs; lower precision raises the compute roof AND the intensity at once.

existing.name: roofline
existing.issue: The core diagram is correct and clear in both themes, but in dark mode the 'flat ceiling - peak FLOP/s' subtitle sits directly on the orange compute-roof line so the words partly overlap the stroke, and the compute-bound annotation box ('prefill, batched decode') crowds the right grid edge, slightly clipping visual breathing room.
existing.improvement: Nudge the 'flat ceiling - peak FLOP/s' subtitle down a few px so it clears the compute-roof line (add ~6-8px vertical clearance and/or shift it left of the roof-label), and give the compute-bound annotation box ~10px more right margin so it doesn't touch the plot's right edge. No structural change — the figure otherwise conveys P(I)=min(pi, beta*I), the ridge, and the two example points excellently.

new_figures.name: intensity-reuse-ladder
new_figures.concept: Why arithmetic intensity differs across ops: it is governed by how many times each byte read from HBM is reused before it is evicted.
new_figures.rationale: The roofline figure shows the x-axis (intensity) and the verdict, but never explains WHY decode/element-wise land left of the ridge and GEMMs land right of it. That 'why' is REUSE, and it is the single most load-bearing intuition in the chapter — it is what FlashAttention, batching, and fusion all exploit. A reader who groks the reuse ladder can predict a kernel's roofline position without a profiler.
new_figures.placement: ### Arithmetic intensity — anchor immediately after the paragraph ending '...swarm of tiny reuse-free element-wise and normalization ops.'
new_figures.brief:
A three-rung 'reuse ladder' read left-to-right (or top-to-bottom), intensity increasing along the reading direction, that turns the abstract I = FLOPs/bytes into a picture of data reuse. THREE panels, each with the same internal template: (1) a small schematic of the operation, (2) ONE highlighted loaded element (a single colored cell) with fan-out arrows showing how many multiply-add operations that one loaded byte feeds before it leaves on-chip memory, (3) a one-line intensity verdict.

Panel A — Element-wise add (z = x + y): draw two short input strips x and y and an output strip z. Highlight one cell x[i]; a single arrow to one '+' to z[i]. Label: 'read x, read y, write z -> ~3 bytes moved per 1 FLOP' and 'I ~= 0.17 FLOP/B, each byte used ONCE.' Tag: 'no reuse -> memory-bound.'

Panel B — Matrix x vector, GEMV (this is decode): draw a tall weight matrix W being streamed row by row into a small activation vector to make a small output vector. Highlight one weight element w; a single arrow to one MAC. Note the activation vector is reused across rows but the weights (the bulk of the bytes) are each touched once. Label: '2dk FLOPs, ~2dk bytes -> I ~= 1 FLOP/B' and 'each WEIGHT used once -> memory-bound. <-- single-stream decode lives here.' Tag: 'streaming, no weight reuse.'

Panel C — Matrix x matrix, GEMM (FFN / prefill): draw a small tile of A loaded into an on-chip SRAM box, and fan-out arrows from that ONE tile to MANY columns of B producing many outputs — visibly more arrows than Panel B. Label: 'one loaded tile reused across n outputs -> I ~= m/3, grows with size' and 'high reuse -> compute-bound.' Tag: 'reuse amortizes the read.'

Across the bottom, a slim shared intensity axis (log) with the ridge I* marked, and the three panels' points (A ~0.17, B ~1, C large) dropped onto it — A and B left of ridge shaded as 'memory roof', C right of ridge as 'compute roof' — explicitly tying this figure back to {{fig:roofline}}. Theme-safe: use accent/accent2/good CSS vars for the memory-vs-compute coding, neutral ink for schematics. ONLY the small illustrative integers/symbols already in the chapter (0.17, ~1, m/3, 2dk); invent no hardware numbers. Everything inside the viewBox, generous whitespace, clear left-to-right reading order. Must be fully legible as a static frame.

new_figures.name: decode-batching-amortization
new_figures.concept: Single-stream decode streams all weights to make ONE token (I~1, on the memory roof); batching B requests reuses one weight read across B tokens (I~B), climbing the roof past the ridge into compute-bound — the entire economic argument for continuous batching.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter calls this 'the entire economic argument for continuous batching' and returns to it in the worked example and the Interview Corner. It is the most consequential applied instance of arithmetic intensity in inference. A two-panel before/after makes the amortization visceral in a way the prose ratio ('per-token weight traffic drops ~64x') cannot: SAME bytes moved, many more useful FLOPs, and a visible slide up the roofline.
new_figures.placement: ## Bytes, Intensity, and the Two Regimes of Inference — anchor after the paragraph ending '...serve many tokens at once — raising the effective intensity.'
new_figures.brief:
Two side-by-side panels sharing a small roofline inset, showing how batching converts a memory-bound GEMV into a compute-bound GEMM WITHOUT changing the bytes moved.

LEFT panel 'single-stream decode (batch = 1)': draw the model as a vertical stack of a few weight-matrix blocks (label 'all weights, ~2N bytes'). A single wide arrow labeled 'stream every weight from HBM' feeds a tiny 1xd activation vector (one thin row) through the stack, producing exactly ONE output token (one small square popping out on the right). Annotate: 'bytes moved = all weights', 'FLOPs = 2N', 'I ~= 1 FLOP/B'. A small roofline inset in the corner shows a dot sitting ON the memory roof, left of the ridge.

RIGHT panel 'batched decode (batch = B)': the SAME weight stack, the SAME single wide 'stream every weight once' arrow (emphasize identical byte cost — perhaps a faint 'same bytes' tie-line between the two panels' arrows), but now a B-row activation block (draw ~4-8 stacked rows for B) flows through, producing B output tokens (B small squares). Annotate: 'bytes moved = all weights (UNCHANGED)', 'FLOPs = B x 2N', 'I ~= B FLOP/B'. Its roofline inset dot has slid UP the slope, reaching/crossing the ridge into the compute-bound region — draw a small arrow on the inset from the left panel's position to this one.

Bottom caption strip: 'One weight read now serves B tokens -> intensity rises ~Bx -> decode crosses the ridge. (Until per-request KV-cache traffic takes over.)' Use a small illustrative B (e.g. B=8), no invented tok/s or bandwidth numbers. Theme-safe via CSS vars (memory-bound = accent2/blue coding, compute-bound = good/green, matching {{fig:roofline}}). Keep the two roofline insets tiny but unmistakably the same axes as the main roofline. Fully understandable as a static frame; if animated, the only motion is the activation rows streaming through and tokens popping out — never required for comprehension. Everything within the viewBox, no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: transformer-flop-anatomy
new_figures.concept: Where a transformer's forward FLOPs live per token/layer: the d^2 terms (FFN 16d^2 + QKVO 8d^2) dominate at moderate context, while attention scores (4sd, ~s^2 over the sequence) are a minority that only overtakes near s ~ 6d — and even then, FLOPs are not runtime.
new_figures.rationale: The FLOP-counting section is the analytical backbone that lets a reader PLACE transformer kernels on the roofline, and it carries two subtle, exam-critical intuitions the prose states but a picture cements: (1) the d^2 terms outweigh attention scores ~12x at typical shapes, with a clean crossover at s ~ 6d, and (2) FLOP share is not runtime share — attention can dominate time while being a FLOP minority because it is memory-bound. A single comparative bar makes both land instantly.
new_figures.placement: ### Attention projections vs. the attention score — anchor after the paragraph ending '...that gap is exactly what the roofline exists to expose.'
new_figures.brief:
A comparative stacked-bar infographic of per-token, per-layer forward FLOPs, showing the three populations and how their proportions shift with context length. Draw TWO horizontal stacked bars of equal total-reference scale:

Bar 1 'moderate context (s small relative to d)': three segments sized to reflect the chapter's decomposition 24d^2 + 4sd — a large FFN segment (16d^2), a medium QKVO-projections segment (8d^2), and a THIN attention-scores sliver (4sd). Label each segment with its symbolic coefficient (16d^2, 8d^2, 4sd), not absolute numbers. Annotate '~12x more projection+FFN FLOPs than attention scores here.'

Bar 2 'long context (s ~ comparable to 6d)': same FFN and QKVO segments (unchanged, they don't depend on s), but the attention-scores segment is now SWOLLEN to roughly match the d^2 terms — visually showing the quadratic term catching up. Annotate the crossover point 'scores overtake the d^2 terms near s ~= 6d.'

Between/around the bars, place a distinct callout badge (different visual weight, e.g. a bordered note): 'FLOPs are NOT runtime: attention scores can dominate TIME even as a FLOP minority, because the score matrix is memory-bound (-> motivates FlashAttention).' This is the load-bearing subtlety.

Use theme-safe CSS-var fills to distinguish the three segments consistently across both bars, with a compact legend (FFN / QKVO projections / attention scores). ONLY symbolic quantities from the chapter (16d^2, 8d^2, 4sd, s~6d); invent no absolute GFLOP numbers. Keep both bars and the badge within the viewBox with clear labels and no overlap; readable as a static frame.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: a single, genuinely strong roofline diagram (keep, with a small dark-mode label-overlap refine). The roofline shows the axes and the verdict but not the underlying intuitions, so I propose three foundational new figures: (1) an arithmetic-intensity REUSE ladder (element-wise vs GEMV vs GEMM) that explains WHY kernels land where they do — the chapter's most load-bearing missing intuition; (2) a decode-vs-batching amortization two-panel, the chapter's central inference 'aha' (same bytes, Bx FLOPs, climb past the ridge); and (3) a transformer FLOP-anatomy comparative bar that cements 'd^2 dominates, s^2 only overtakes near s~6d, and FLOPs are not runtime.' Priorities 1/1/2 respectively.
